metadata.title: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. TEAL = complex / phase / real winding; CRIMSON = probability density / |psi|^2 / real-only attempt
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Portuguese. Medhavy 1-word budget. Wagwan is Bear-only.
metadata.audio_note: All existing inner-beat mp3s were generated with ElevenLabs NBB voice. Must regenerate ALL with kokoro af_kore for Medhavy: python3 runtime/scripts/generate_audio.py quantum-mechanics-vol1/youtube/vox-complex-wavefunction

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A right-moving and a left-moving packet can share the exact same probability density. The wave function is complex, winding one way for rightward motion and the other for leftward. Squaring to get the probability discards the winding direction, so the clouds look identical. Real-only waves are always two opposite-moving pieces; the imaginary part is where momentum and direction live.

props.artifactTitle: Why the wave function is complex
artifactLines: A right-moving and a left-moving packet can share the exact same probability density.
The wave function is complex, winding one way for rightward motion and the other for leftward.
Squaring to get the probability discards the winding direction, so the clouds look identical.
Real-only waves are always two opposite-moving pieces; the imaginary part is where momentum and direction live.

beats.narration_text: The helix picture fixed it for me: right-movers wind clockwise, left-movers counterclockwise, and squaring to get the probability throws that winding away, so real-only waves are always secretly two-directional. I want to prove it to myself. Please: (1) write the rightward and leftward plane waves and show their identical modulus, (2) demonstrate that any purely real packet decomposes into equal opposite-moving pieces, and (3) explain why the i in the Schrodinger equation is what breaks the forward-backward time symmetry.

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: The helix picture fixed it for me: right-movers wind clockwise, left-movers counterclockwise, and squaring to get the probability throws that winding away, so real-only waves are always secretly two-directional. I want to prove it to myself. Please: (1) write the rightward and leftward plane waves and show their identical modulus, (2) demonstrate that any purely real packet decomposes into equal opposite-moving pieces, and (3) explain why the i in the Schrodinger equation is what breaks the forward-backward time symmetry.

beats.narration_text: Take a wave packet moving right and one moving left. Their probability clouds look identical. The thing that tells them apart is hiding in the imaginary part.

card.copy: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two Gaussian probability density humps side by side, one labeled 'moving right' and one 'moving left', both identical in shape"

beats.narration_text: Measure the probability density of an electron moving right, then one moving left at the same speed. The histograms match exactly — both are the same Gaussian hump.
Classical physics says position tells you everything. But these two particles are different — one is racing right, the other left. The probability density is blind to that.

card.copy: Two identical probability humps. One moves right, one moves left. How does the wave function know which is which?

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two complex wave function helices in 3D, one winding clockwise (rightward momentum) and one counterclockwise (leftward momentum), labeled with winding direction"

beats.narration_text: The wave function is a complex number at every point — a length and a direction. If you plot the real and imaginary parts as a helix in space, the helix winds clockwise for rightward motion and counterclockwise for leftward motion.

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: show |psi|^2 squaring operation discarding winding direction — helix collapses to its length profile, both left and right movers produce the same hump"

beats.narration_text: The probability density |psi| squared only uses the length of that complex number — it loses the winding direction entirely. Both particles have the same length profile, so they look the same.

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a real-valued wave packet decomposing into two symmetric counter-propagating waves, showing it cannot represent a single direction of travel"

beats.narration_text: Now try to build a wave packet from only real numbers. A real wave is a symmetric standing wave — it can't represent a single direction of travel. Any real packet is secretly two waves moving in opposite directions at once.

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: the phase winding rate of a complex wave function as a helix, with an arrow showing the momentum direction encoded in the winding — label the winding rate as k (momentum)"

beats.narration_text: The imaginary part is the only place where direction of motion lives. The phase — the winding rate of the helix — is the momentum. Without the imaginary part, momentum is ambiguous.

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: side-by-side comparison of Schrodinger equation with i vs without i — show time evolution going only forward with i, symmetric forward/backward without it"

beats.narration_text: This is why the Schrodinger equation uses i on the left side — it needs to distinguish future from past and right-mover from left-mover. Without i, time evolution would be symmetric forward and backward and every moving state would be its own mirror image.

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a Gaussian wave packet with k0=5 per nanometer winding clockwise, then flip to k0=-5 winding counterclockwise — show both probability densities overlaid as identical humps"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: a Gaussian packet with momentum k-zero equals 5 per nanometer. The phase winds at 5 cycles per nanometer — clockwise viewed from the front. Flip k-zero to minus 5 and the helix flips. The probability density is identical in both cases.
The real part shows where the particle is. The imaginary part shows where it is going. Strip away the imaginary part and you can't tell left from right.

card.copy: Real part: where it is. Imaginary part: where it is going. Strip the imaginary part and you lose the direction.

beats.narration_text: Take that imaginary part and make it yours. Paste this into Claude and see what direction looks like in the wavefunction.

props.segment: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
props.command: claude "show me two wave packets with identical probability densities but opposite momenta, and explain what the imaginary part of ψ encodes"

beats.narration_text: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
